cards.front: How many standard amino acids are used to build proteins?
Which stereochemical configuration do all standard proteinogenic amino acids share?
Which is the only standard amino acid that is achiral?
Which amino acid has a secondary amine and forms a rigid ring with its backbone?
Which three amino acids are aromatic?
Which two amino acids contain sulfur in their side chains?
Which sulfur-containing amino acid can form disulfide bonds?
Which three amino acids are positively charged (basic) at physiological pH?
Which two amino acids are negatively charged (acidic) at physiological pH?
Which basic amino acid side chain contains an imidazole ring?
Which basic amino acid side chain contains a guanidinium group?
Which amino acid side chains contain a hydroxyl group?
What is the one-letter code for tryptophan?
What is the one-letter code for glutamate?
What is the one-letter code for glutamine?
What is the one-letter code for lysine?
What is the one-letter code for phenylalanine?
What is the three-letter code for asparagine?
The amino acid coded by the start codon AUG is which residue?
A zwitterion carries what type of net charge?
What is the isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid?
cards.back: The pH at which it has no net charge

cards.front: For an amino acid with only one acidic and one basic group, how is the pI calculated?
cards.back: Average of its two pKa values

cards.front: Amino acids with a nonpolar side chain tend to be located where in a folded globular protein?
Charged, hydrophilic residues tend to be located where in a folded globular protein?
Which amino acid frequently disrupts alpha helices and introduces kinks?
What functional group defines the carboxyl-terminus of a polypeptide?
In what direction is a polypeptide chain conventionally written?
What type of covalent bond links amino acids in a polypeptide?
What small molecule is released when a peptide bond forms?
The partial double-bond character of the peptide bond arises from what?
cards.back: Resonance/delocalization of electrons across the amide

cards.front: What is the primary structure of a protein?
cards.back: The linear sequence of amino acids

cards.front: Name the two most common types of secondary structure.
cards.back: Alpha helix and beta pleated sheet

cards.front: Which bonds stabilize protein secondary structure?
What is tertiary structure?
cards.back: The overall three-dimensional folding of a single polypeptide

cards.front: What is quaternary structure?
cards.back: The assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits into one protein

cards.front: Which level of protein structure involves multiple separate polypeptide chains?
Which covalent bond helps stabilize tertiary structure and forms between two cysteines?
What type of reaction forms a disulfide bond?
What type of reaction cleaves a disulfide bond?
Anfinsen's ribonuclease experiment demonstrated that a protein's tertiary structure is determined by what?
What is the loss of a protein's native three-dimensional structure called?
Name two agents commonly used to denature proteins.
cards.back: Heat and urea (also extreme pH, detergents)

cards.front: Which protein contains a heme prosthetic group and consists of a single polypeptide?
How many subunits does hemoglobin have?
Which oxygen-binding protein displays cooperative (sigmoidal) binding?
What is a non-amino-acid molecule permanently and tightly bound to a protein called?
The motor protein that walks toward the plus (+) end of microtubules is called what?
The motor protein that moves toward the minus (-) end of microtubules is called what?
Actin monomers at the (+) end are typically bound to which nucleotide?
What is the term for actin filament growth at the (+) end matched by shrinkage at the (-) end?
Which antibody class is secreted as a pentamer in the early immune response?
Which antibody class predominates in mucosal secretions?
Which antibody binds allergens and triggers histamine release?
Which region of an antibody binds the antigen?
By what general term is a biological catalyst that is a protein described?
Enzymes increase reaction rate by lowering what quantity?
Do enzymes change the equilibrium constant (Keq) of a reaction?
Do enzymes change the free energy (deltaG) of a reaction?
Which molecular species does an enzyme's active site bind most tightly?
Which model describes an active site that changes shape upon substrate binding?
Which model describes a rigid, complementary active site?
Enzymes that catalyze the transfer of a phosphate group are called what?
Enzymes that catalyze oxidation-reduction reactions are called what?
Enzymes that rearrange atoms within a molecule are called what?
Enzymes that remove a phosphate group are called what?
Enzymes that join two molecules using ATP are classified as what?
What enzyme catalyzes the interconversion of CO2 and water with carbonic acid?
In Michaelis-Menten kinetics, what does Vmax represent?
cards.back: The maximum reaction rate at saturating substrate

cards.front: What does the Michaelis constant Km equal in terms of reaction rate?
cards.back: The substrate concentration at half of Vmax

cards.front: A low Km indicates what about an enzyme's affinity for its substrate?
How does a competitive inhibitor affect apparent Km?
How does a competitive inhibitor affect Vmax?
Where does a competitive inhibitor bind?
How does a pure noncompetitive inhibitor affect Vmax?
How does a pure noncompetitive inhibitor affect Km?
Competitive inhibition can be overcome by increasing what?
An uncompetitive inhibitor binds only to which complex?
How does an uncompetitive inhibitor affect both Km and Vmax?
What kind of plot linearizes Michaelis-Menten data as a double-reciprocal graph?
On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the y-intercept equals what?
On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the x-intercept equals what?
Regulation of an enzyme by a molecule binding away from the active site is called what?
When a pathway's final product inhibits an early enzyme, this is called what?
Enzymes displaying cooperative, sigmoidal kinetics are said to be what type?
Adding phosphate groups to modify enzyme activity is called what covalent modification?
A small organic molecule that assists enzyme catalysis and is loosely bound is called what?
Inorganic ions that assist enzyme function are called what?
The full catalytically active enzyme with its cofactor is called what?
An enzyme lacking its required cofactor is called what?
Which digestive protease is secreted by the stomach?
Carboxypeptidase cleaves peptide bonds at which end of a protein?
What is the general term for an inactive enzyme precursor?
Which functional group determines whether a monosaccharide is an aldose or a ketose?
What is the molecular formula of glucose?
Which carbon's configuration distinguishes D from L sugars in Fischer projection?
Which sugars are used by human metabolism, D or L?
The six-membered cyclic form of a sugar is called a what?
The five-membered cyclic form of a sugar is called a what?
What is the new stereocenter formed when a sugar cyclizes called?
The two cyclic forms differing only at the anomeric carbon are called what?
Sugars that differ in configuration at a single non-anomeric carbon are called what?
The spontaneous interconversion of alpha and beta anomers in solution is called what?
A sugar with a free anomeric carbon that can reduce other agents is called a what?
Which disaccharide is composed of glucose and fructose?
Which disaccharide is composed of glucose and galactose?
Which disaccharide is composed of two glucose units?
Is sucrose a reducing or non-reducing sugar?
Which enzyme hydrolyzes sucrose into its monosaccharides?
What glycosidic linkage joins glucose units in cellulose?
What glycosidic linkage joins glucose units in the linear portions of starch and glycogen?
What glycosidic linkage forms the branch points in glycogen?
Why is glycogen highly branched?
cards.back: To maximize non-reducing ends for rapid glucose release

cards.front: Which polysaccharide is the main storage form of glucose in animals?
What are the three fatty acid ester bonds of a triacylglycerol attached to?
Which have higher melting points, saturated or unsaturated fatty acids?
Cis double bonds in fatty acid tails have what effect on membrane fluidity?
What amphipathic lipid class forms the bulk of cell membranes?
In a phospholipid, which part is hydrophilic?
Which structural lipid increases membrane rigidity at high temperature and fluidity at low temperature?
Which lipid class serves as the precursor for steroid hormones?
The reaction that hydrolyzes a triacylglycerol with a strong base to yield soap is called what?
The self-assembly of amphipathic lipids in water is driven by which effect?
What model describes the cell membrane as a dynamic lipid bilayer with embedded proteins?
Which lipoprotein transports dietary lipids from the intestine?
Which lipoprotein is known as 'good cholesterol' and returns cholesterol to the liver?
Which lipoprotein delivers cholesterol to tissues and is called 'bad cholesterol'?
Which lipoprotein carries the highest proportion of triacylglycerols made by the liver?
Fat-soluble vitamins are which four?
Water-soluble vitamins include which two groups?
cards.back: The B vitamins and vitamin C

cards.front: Which class of vitamins can accumulate to toxic levels because they are stored in fat?
Which nitrogenous bases are purines?
Which nitrogenous bases are pyrimidines?
Which base is found in RNA but not DNA?
Which base is found in DNA but not RNA?
How many hydrogen bonds form between guanine and cytosine?
How many hydrogen bonds form between adenine and thymine?
A nucleoside plus one or more phosphate groups is called a what?
Which sugar is found in DNA?
Which sugar is found in RNA?
At which carbon does deoxyribose lack a hydroxyl relative to ribose?
The backbone of a nucleic acid strand is composed of what two alternating units?
The linkage joining nucleotides in a nucleic acid backbone is called what?
In which direction is a new DNA strand synthesized?
The two strands of the DNA double helix are oriented how relative to each other?
DNA-rich regions with many G-C pairs have a higher or lower melting temperature?
Which interactions between adjacent bases stabilize the DNA double helix in addition to hydrogen bonds?
In which cellular compartment does glycolysis occur?
Does glycolysis require oxygen?
What is the net ATP yield of glycolysis per glucose?
How many ATP are invested in the early (energy investment) phase of glycolysis?
What is the end product of glycolysis (per three-carbon molecule)?
How many pyruvate molecules are produced per glucose in glycolysis?
How many NADH are produced per glucose in glycolysis?
Which enzyme is the rate-limiting (committed) step of glycolysis?
Which enzyme phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate in most cells?
Which liver-specific isozyme phosphorylates glucose and has a high Km?
High levels of ATP have what effect on phosphofructokinase-1?
Which glycolytic enzyme converts PEP to pyruvate?
Name the three irreversible enzymes of glycolysis.
In glycolysis, ATP produced by direct phosphate transfer to ADP is called what?
Under anaerobic conditions in human muscle, pyruvate is converted to what?
What is the purpose of fermentation in regenerating a coenzyme?
cards.back: To regenerate NAD+ so glycolysis can continue

cards.front: In yeast, anaerobic fermentation of pyruvate produces which two products?
Which human cell relies exclusively on glycolysis because it lacks mitochondria?
Which enzyme complex converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA?
In which cellular compartment does the pyruvate dehydrogenase reaction occur?
What gas is released when pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA?
The conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA produces which electron carrier?
How many carbons does acetyl-CoA contribute to the citric acid cycle?
Another common name for the citric acid cycle is what?
In which cellular compartment does the citric acid cycle occur?
Acetyl-CoA combines with which four-carbon molecule to start the citric acid cycle?
What six-carbon product forms in the first step of the citric acid cycle?
Which enzyme catalyzes the first step of the citric acid cycle?
How many NADH are produced per turn of the citric acid cycle?
How many FADH2 are produced per turn of the citric acid cycle?
How many CO2 molecules are released per turn of the citric acid cycle?
What high-energy nucleotide is produced by substrate-level phosphorylation in the citric acid cycle?
Which citric acid cycle enzyme is also part of the electron transport chain and reduces FAD?
Where is the electron transport chain located?
Which molecule is the final electron acceptor of the electron transport chain?
The final product formed when oxygen accepts electrons in the ETC is what?
At which ETC complex does NADH donate its electrons?
At which ETC complex does FADH2 donate its electrons?
Which mobile lipid carrier shuttles electrons to Complex III?
Which mobile protein carries electrons from Complex III to Complex IV?
What ion gradient does the electron transport chain build across the inner membrane?
The theory linking the proton gradient to ATP synthesis is called what?
Which enzyme uses the proton gradient to synthesize ATP?
Approximately how many ATP does one NADH yield via oxidative phosphorylation?
Approximately how many ATP does one FADH2 yield via oxidative phosphorylation?
A molecule that dissipates the proton gradient without ATP synthesis is called a what?
The synthesis of new glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors is called what?
In which two organs does gluconeogenesis primarily occur?
Gluconeogenesis bypasses which three irreversible steps of glycolysis?
cards.back: Those catalyzed by pyruvate kinase, PFK-1, and hexokinase

cards.front: Which enzyme converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate in gluconeogenesis?
Which enzyme hydrolyzes fructose-1,6-bisphosphate in gluconeogenesis?
The breakdown of glycogen to release glucose is called what?
The synthesis of glycogen is called what?
Which enzyme cleaves glucose units from glycogen using inorganic phosphate?
What product results when glycogen phosphorylase removes a glucose unit?
Which enzyme adds glucose units to a growing glycogen chain?
Which hormone stimulates glycogen breakdown and gluconeogenesis during fasting?
Which hormone promotes glucose uptake and glycogen synthesis after a meal?
Which hormone mediates the fight-or-flight stimulation of glycogenolysis?
The oxidation of fatty acids to acetyl-CoA is called what?
In which cellular compartment does fatty acid beta-oxidation occur?
Each round of beta-oxidation shortens the fatty acid chain by how many carbons?
Each round of beta-oxidation produces which three products?
cards.back: One acetyl-CoA, one NADH, and one FADH2

cards.front: How many acetyl-CoA molecules are produced from complete beta-oxidation of an 18-carbon fatty acid?
Which molecule carries fatty acids across the inner mitochondrial membrane?
Compared to carbohydrates, do fatty acids yield more or less ATP per gram?
The liver produces which fuel molecules from acetyl-CoA during prolonged fasting?
Which cellular process synthesizes fatty acids in the cytosol?
The toxic nitrogenous waste from amino acid catabolism is converted to what for excretion?
The transfer of an amino group from an amino acid to a keto acid is catalyzed by which enzyme?
The first law of thermodynamics states that energy is what?
A reaction with a negative deltaG is described as what?
A reaction with a positive deltaG is described as what?
Cells drive nonspontaneous reactions by linking them to ATP hydrolysis; this is called what?
Which cellular molecule is the primary energy currency?
Hydrolysis of ATP most commonly yields which two products?
When ATP is cleaved to AMP, what byproduct is released?
Which electron carrier is reduced by accepting a hydride ion to become NADH?
Which flavin electron carrier is reduced to FADH2?
Which factors affect the rate of a reaction but not its equilibrium?
Le Chatelier's principle predicts a system shifts to counteract changes in what?
The overall aerobic catabolism of glucose yields approximately how many ATP?
Which lab technique separates charged molecules by size and charge in an electric field?
In SDS-PAGE, what does the detergent SDS do to proteins?
cards.back: Denatures them and gives a uniform negative charge

cards.front: In SDS-PAGE, proteins are separated primarily by what property?
In SDS-PAGE, do smaller or larger proteins migrate farther through the gel?
What reagent is added to SDS-PAGE to reduce disulfide bonds?
Which stain is commonly used to visualize protein bands on a gel?
At what pH will a protein not migrate in electrophoresis?
Which chromatography technique separates molecules by size, eluting the largest first?
Which chromatography technique separates molecules based on net charge?
Which chromatography technique separates using a specific binding partner like an antibody?
In reversed-phase chromatography, which molecules elute last?
The Rf value in chromatography is the ratio of distance traveled by the compound to that by what?
Which blotting technique detects a specific protein using antibodies?
Which blotting technique detects a specific DNA sequence?
Which blotting technique detects a specific RNA sequence?
Which antibody-based assay quantifies a protein or antigen using enzyme-linked detection?
Which technique amplifies specific DNA sequences using cycles of heating and cooling?
Which heat-stable DNA polymerase is used in PCR?
Which divalent cation is essential in the PCR reaction buffer?
During which PCR phase are the two DNA strands separated by heat?
Short DNA sequences that define the region amplified in PCR are called what?
Which DNA sequencing method uses chain-terminating dideoxynucleotides?
A cDNA library is generated from mRNA using which enzyme?
A cDNA library contains only which sequences of a genome?
Enzymes that cut DNA at specific palindromic recognition sequences are called what?
Which enzyme joins DNA fragments together by forming phosphodiester bonds?
The eukaryotic mRNA feature that poly-thymine probes hybridize to is called what?
A protein's absorbance and extinction coefficient are related to concentration by which law?
